narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as such a steep uphill climb—90.6% likelihood of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school, the single biggest red flag that whispered "struggle ahead" louder than anything else, as students in that spot rarely power through to bar success. Layered on that was your LSAT score of 27, which the model flagged as a strong pull toward failure, signaling the kind of foundational hurdles that have tripped up many before you. Your Year 1 decile of 1 compounded this, painting a picture of early and persistent academic challenges rather than a upward trajectory. Even your middle-family income, while solid for many walks of life, didn't offer the extra buffers—like tutoring or networks—that often help others in law school grind past these lows. Your Asian background, unfortunately, aligned in the model's view with profiles that face steeper odds in this arena, adding another layer of caution. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 tugged in the same direction, reinforcing that law school might demand a gear you haven't fully hit yet. As a full-time female student, these traits together wove a narrative of high risk, where the deck statistically feels stacked against crossing the bar finish line. It's not about your potential or heart—it's about these combined signals from your profile matching stories that ended in heartbreak far too often. If you pour targeted effort into boosting those grades and test prep, though, you could rewrite this script entirely. Hang in there; your story isn't over.
